answer:
**Iqbal's Attack on Territorial Nationalism in *Wataniyat***  

Iqbal portrays *wataniyat* (territorial nationalism) as a modern Western invention—a false idol that desecrates Islamic faith and universal brotherhood by reducing "homeland" to mere physical land.[^1] He writes:  

> Of all these newer gods, the greatest is the Nation;  
> The robe it wears is Faith’s own final desecration.[^2]  

This "idol, forged by this new age’s philosophy," wastes the "Prophet’s house of faith and piety," sowing rivalry among peoples, promoting conquest over trade, and severing "the roots of Islam’s brotherhood."[^3] Critically, Iqbal contrasts secular nationalism's narrow territorialism—"In the discourse of the state, the Nation is but land"—with prophetic teaching, where "Homeland is by God’s command," implying a supranational, divine ummah unbound by geography.[^4] "To be confined to one place is calamity," he urges; Muslims should "live free within the ocean, like a fish at sea."[^5] Thus, territorial nationalism is a colonial "hollow idol" to be ground to dust.[^6]  

**Dunoyer's Grounding of Liberty in Race, Climate, and Territory**  

In *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, Dunoyer identifies three foundational conditions of liberty: *race* (organic endowments), *external circumstances* (climate, soil fertility, terrain disposition), and *culture* (developed faculties).[^7] These natural preconditions shape a society's capacity for free industrial action before culture refines them. Race provides innate organs/faculties; external circumstances (e.g., "heat, humidity, cold, [soil] fertility or sterility, disposition of waters and lands") either favor or hinder force development; culture then perfects what nature endows.[^8] Liberty emerges progressively as societies adapt to these—nomadism suits arid climates/territories better than sedentary life, etc.—but always rooted in race/climate/soil as "primitive" enablers.[^9] Without favorable territory/soil/climate, even cultural efforts falter; e.g., sterile soil limits vegetative/agricultural forces essential to production.[^10]  

**Exact Point of Iqbal's Critique Against Dunoyer**  

Iqbal's argument cuts directly against Dunoyer's naturalistic grounding of liberty in *territorial conditions* (soil/land/climate) by rejecting *any* confinement of human/Islamic destiny to geography as idolatrous and colonial. Dunoyer treats territory as a liberty-enabling precondition ("the soil... is but a capital," vital for forces like vegetation/animal life, shaping societal progress from savagery onward).[^11] Iqbal counters: the state's "Nation is but land," a "calamity" that divides God's creation into rival territories, versus Islam's universal "Homeland... by God’s command"—transcending race/climate/soil.[^12] Where Dunoyer sees territorial adaptation as liberty's base (e.g., nomads thriving in harsh lands via race/circumstances), Iqbal deems it a "new age" desecration, chaining the soul to material bounds and severing brotherhood.[^13] Iqbal thus inverts Dunoyer's hierarchy: true liberty rejects territorial/racial/climatic determinism for divine universality.  

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 25 (*Wataniyat*), [full chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/).  
[^2]: *Ibid.*, [p-79b1e9](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-79b1e9).  
[^3]: *Ibid.*, [p-2737d5](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-2737d5); [p-97937b](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-97937b).  
[^4]: *Ibid.*, [p-a723b2](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-a723b2).  
[^5]: *Ibid.*  
[^6]: *Ibid.*, [p-2737d5](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-2737d5).  
[^7]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, ch. 13, [p-c248a1](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/13-iii-des-divers-ordres-de-travaux-et-de-fonctions-q/translation/#p-c248a1).  
[^8]: *Ibid.*; cf. *VOL I*, ch. 3, [p-a30dd3](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/03-ii-influence-des-circonstances-exterieures-sur-la/translation/#p-a30dd3) (external: "soil... fertility or sterility").  
[^9]: *VOL II*, ch. 13, [p-c248a1](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/13-iii-des-divers-ordres-de-travaux-et-de-fonctions-q/translation/#p-c248a1); *VOL I*, ch. 2, [p-0027be](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/02-i-influence-de-la-race-sur-la-liberte/translation/#p-0027be).  
[^10]: *VOL II*, ch. 17, [p-97e999](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/17-vii-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-agricole-130/translation/#p-97e999); ch. 14, [p-5561a7](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/14-iv-des-conditions-auxquelles-toute-industrie-peut-/translation/#p-5561a7).  
[^11]: *VOL II*, ch. 14, [p-5561a7](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/14-iv-des-conditions-auxquelles-toute-industrie-peut-/translation/#p-5561a7).  
[^12]: Iqbal, *Wataniyat*, [p-a723b2](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-a723b2).  
[^13]: Dunoyer, *VOL II*, ch. 13, [p-c248a1](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/13-iii-des-divers-ordres-de-travaux-et-de-fonctions-q/translation/#p-c248a1); Iqbal, [p-97937b](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-97937b).
